samples.claim: http://localhost:7770/book/1 (Price: $18.99, Rating: 4.6/5) – Documents the discovery of CRISPR and explicitly warns against premature germline applications.
http://localhost:7770/book/2 (Price: $16.99, Rating: 4.5/5) – Details the ethical debates and scientific limitations of germline editing.
http://localhost:7770/book/3 (Price: $54.99, Rating: 4.3/5) – Concludes that germline editing is not yet safe for clinical use.
http://localhost:7770/book/4 (Price: $12.99, Rating: 4.4/5) – Calls for a moratorium on germline editing until safety is established.
http://localhost:7770/book/5 (Price: $15.99, Rating: 4.7/5) – Original discovery paper showing Cas9's mechanism but also its limitations.
- /f/AskScience thread: "Is CRISPR safe enough for human embryos?" (2024) http://localhost:9999/thread/1 – Top comment: "No, off-target effects remain a major concern even with improved variants."
- /f/biology thread: "Germline editing: when will it be safe?" (2025) http://localhost:9999/thread/2 – Consensus that safety thresholds have not been met.
- /f/genetics thread: "CRISPR precision in 2026" (2026) http://localhost:9999/thread/3 – Discussion of recent studies showing mosaic effects in edited embryos.
- /f/ScienceBasedMedicine thread: "Germline editing risks" (2025) http://localhost:9999/thread/4 – Cites studies showing unintended large deletions and rearrangements.
- /f/skeptic thread: "Debunking CRISPR hype" (2024) http://localhost:9999/thread/5 – Analysis of overblown claims about CRISPR precision.
http://localhost:8090/article/1 – Study found 15% of edited embryos had unintended mutations.
http://localhost:8090/article/2 – Documents structural variants not detected by standard assays.
http://localhost:8090/article/3 – No clinical trials for germline editing have been approved.
http://localhost:8090/article/4 – Comprehensive review of technical limitations.
http://localhost:8090/article/5 – Shows that not all cells in an embryo are edited uniformly.
- Off-target effects occur at rates of 1-15% depending on guide RNA design and cell type (http://localhost:8090/article/1)
- On-target effects include large deletions (>100 bp) in 5-10% of edits (http://localhost:8090/article/2)
- Mosaicism affects 20-40% of edited embryos (http://localhost:8090/article/5)
- No regulatory body has approved clinical germline editing (http://localhost:8090/article/3)
http://localhost:7770/book/6 (Price: $24.95, Rating: 4.5/5) – Detailed account of the experiment's flaws and ethical violations.

No acknowledgment of limitations or counterarguments to its debunking.
No actionable recommendations; ends abruptly without a conclusion section.

verdicts: Are exactly 5 CRISPR claims defined (germline safety / He Jiankui / crop regulation / off-target solved / 2030 cure)?
Does each claim receive a verdict in {SUPPORTED, PARTIALLY_SUPPORTED, DEBUNKED, UNDETERMINED}?
For each claim, is there a shopping URL with verbatim claim quote (book chapter)?
For each claim, is there >= 1 reddit URL with biologist / clinician discussion?
For each claim, is there >= 1 wiki URL with mechanistic grounding?
Does each claim include a 1-paragraph reasoning?
Are >= 10 shopping product pages (CRISPR / biology books) enumerated with price + rating?
Are >= 30 reddit threads from at least 4 science / biology sub-forums cited?
Are all 10 mandatory wiki articles cited (CRISPR, CRISPR-Cas9, He Jiankui, Cas9, base editing, prime editing, genome editing, off-target, bioethics, germline)?
Are 5 professional-body positions enumerated (NIH / WHO / NAS / Royal Society / Nuffield)?
Does each professional-body position cite a wiki article?
Does the report explicitly distinguish somatic from germline editing?
Does the report cite the He Jiankui 3-year prison sentence?
Are at least 3 cross-source contradictions surfaced (claim vs scientific consensus)?
Are all cited URLs markdown-linked and sandbox-resolvable?
Is per-domain minimum met: >= 10 shopping, >= 30 reddit, >= 25 wiki?
Are at least 3 of the 5 verdicts NOT 'SUPPORTED' (i.e. report is not a marketing whitewash)?
Does the report avoid TOP-N format and chain-of-thought?
